data.title: Triple-B Problems In The Debt Market
data.description:
Sometimes the wry humor just writes itself. This week saw the House pass a tax bill going by the name “one big beautiful bill” – or “Triple-B”, as some of the Republican House staffers would refer to it in their back-and-forth messaging during the sausage-making process.

Bond investors, always happy to latch onto a joke at some else’s expense, piled onto the Triple-B moniker – BBB, of course, is the lowest rung on the investment grade credit scale before descending into the funhouse of junk bond ratings.

As in: that’s where American government debt – the world’s long-enduring proxy for a risk-free rate – could eventually be headed if this bill makes its way into law. Passage is not yet a foregone conclusion, as the bill will have to make its way past some potentially ornery opposition in the Senate before it gets to the president’s desk.

But given the current state of Republican politics and the administration’s overweening desire to see this thing to the finish line, it’s a pretty good bet that something very close to the current plan will manage to arrive at 1600 Pennsylvania before too long.

20-year Bond? Anyone?

The latest opposing salvo from the bond market came on Wednesday this week when a $16 billion auction of 20-year Treasury bonds, in the face of weak demand, went out at a yield of 5 percent. The 20-year maturity is a relatively recent addition to the Treasury stable, introduced in 2020, and this week’s auction represented the highest yield for a new offering.

On the same day as the tepid 20-year offering, yields on the bellwether 30-year bond reached their highest level since 2007. At the same time the dollar, which had been finding some strength in recent days thanks mostly to the apparent de-escalation of the trade war, resumed falling, and conditions started to look a lot like those dark days in the immediate wake of the April 2 tariff announcements (as we are writing this on Friday morning, those kinder and gentler trade headlines of late are coming under renewed fire with a new announcement by the administration of 50 percent tariffs on products from the EU and 25 percent tariffs specifically on any Apple iPhones not made in the US. Ugh.).

Gently, then Suddenly

To be clear, the bond market’s reaction this week has been more of a gentle nudge than a violent shove. Yields on intermediate and long-dated maturities have settled back down a bit from where they were in the immediate aftermath of the 20-year auction on Wednesday.

There may be some hedging going on as investors speculate on whether there might be more meaningful pushback from the Senate before the Triple-B tax act gets written into law (a sentiment we do not share, given that the main force likely to push up debt and attendant deficits will come from the extension of the 2017 tax cuts, and we see little to no likelihood of those being pulled out or tampered down by anyone in this Congress).

In general, movements in the bond market tend to be more measured and gradual than the more volatile day-to-day lurches we often see in the stock market.

Our concern, though, is that at a certain tipping point things can start to unravel quickly. What might that tipping point be? Some observers think that if the 10-year breaches five percent, then we could see a much more pronounced pullback, along with plummeting demand for new Treasury issues, making this week’s 20-year auction seem quaint by comparison.

We’re not convinced that five percent – which is only 0.5 percent away from where yields are today – would be that dramatic of a trigger. We agree, though, that there is an event horizon out there beyond which we have no desire to travel.

That famous line from Ernest Hemingway’s “The Sun Also Rises” comes to mind, when a character named Bill asks another, Mike, how he went bankrupt. Mike replies “gradually, then suddenly.”

We hope that our policymakers will heed the bond market’s gentle nudges and take appropriate actions before they turn into shoves of a more sudden and unpleasant nature. Meanwhile, our bond market mantra is simple: keep your credit quality high, diversify where possible, and think shorter duration.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Morgan Stanley Direct Lending: Is This 10% Yield Safe?
data.description:
Roughly a year ago, we put out an article titled "Blackstone Secured Lending: Is This 10% Yield Sustainable?".

In that article, we took a look at BXSL, one of the leading 'business development companies' on the market. With billions in assets, a diversified portfolio, and a well-covered 10% dividend, we were positive on the stock, although at the time we thought the valuation looked a tad expensive. Ultimately, we rated the stock a 'Hold'.

Since that article, BXSL has gone to outperform the S&P 500 by a ratio of more than 3-1, returning ~10% since last July versus the S&Ps return of only 3.1%:

While we’re traditionally focused on growth opportunities and opportunistic option trades here at PropNotes, over the last year or so we’ve begun to warm significantly on the prospects of business development companies - or 'BDCs' for short.

Firms like BXSL take investor capital and debt financing, and invest into highly secure loans to stable, middle-market companies. Not only does this provide a needed liquidity function within the broader American economy, but this credit class - in particular - tends to pay reasonable, stable returns over time, making it ideal for income investors.

In this article, we wanted to take a look at Morgan Stanley Direct Lending (NYSE:MSDL), another externally managed BDC.

Similar to BXSL, MSDL invests primarily in secured credit lent out to middle market companies. With a portfolio of more than 200 outstanding loans, billions and assets, and an attractive risk profile - the question remains: is MSDL a good investment?

Will shares in this popular BDC produce robust income to investors over the long term, or is this 10% yield nothing but a mirage?

Today, we’ll take a deeper look at MSDL, break down the company's portfolio, and make the case that this BDC deserves a spot in your portfolio.

Sounds good? Let’s dive in.

Financials

As we mentioned before, MSDL - similar to most business development companies - is in the business of lending. That means that primarily all of the company's revenues and earnings come from assets on the firm's balance sheet.

As it stands right now, MSDL has - in our opinion - a well-constructed, low risk, diverse portfolio. With more than 210 individual borrowers no single loan concentration making up more than 1.9% of total capital, the company isn’t directly exposed to any one company or industry disproportionately:

For reference, the largest industry concentration is in Software, which has a 19.5% allocation.

On the credit side, almost all of MSDL’s $3.8 billion portfolio is trading within expected risk parameters, with small dribs and drabs either outperforming or underperforming expectations. At present, only 0.1% of the portfolio - or roughly $3.4 million - of the loans are internally classed with a risk grading of '4', which implies that recouping principle could be very difficult going forward:

Finally, when you’re looking at where these loans sit on the borrower's capital stack, more than 96% of all of this outstanding credit are first lien investments, which indicates a very conservative approach to lending and a very secure income stream for MSDL in the future.

Overall, we'd argue that MSDL’s portfolio of outstanding credit is incredibly well diversified, secure and performant. When you lay on top the fact that 95% of the loans made are to non-cyclical industries (at least in Morgan Stanley’s opinion) it’s hard to call this asset base anything other than bulletproof, even in the event of considerable economic turbulence.

On the financing side, as we mentioned earlier, MSDL also takes out loans from lenders itself to help finance some of this portfolio. Obviously, MSDL pays a lower rate to borrow funds that it lends out at, which creates a nice credit spread for equity investors.

As we see it, the financing strategy MSDL has chosen reflects a conservative approach, with less than half of the outstanding debt being secured against the company's assets. Additionally, with a blended interest rate of only around 6%, MSDL is able to borrow at an incredibly low rate, which juices the credit spreads earned:

Taken together, between the outstanding positions and debt, we're big fans of MSDL's portfolio, and expect that the company should be able to produce consistent net income through almost all market cycles going forward.

Yield

But how is the company actually performed?

Is MSDL going to be able to continue paying a 10% dividend into the future?

We expect so, and here’s why.

If you look at MSDL’s earnings, you’ll find the total investment income typically comes in at around 100 million per quarter. Annually, this implies investment incomes in the range of $400 million to $420 million, which backs out to a run rate of roughly ~11% blended interest. These numbers have been incredibly consistent over the years, which lays a strong foundation for future dividend payouts:

When you shave off interest, management fees, and incentives, you end up with net investment income in the $50 million range, although this has dipped in recent quarters as rates have fallen. If you back this net investment income number into a per-share figure, you’ll see that MSDL earns somewhere in the range of $0.50-$0.60 per share, per quarter.

Given that MSDL pays a regular dividend of roughly $0.50 per quarter (and occasionally has extra income to send out supplemental distributions), it’s clear to us that MSDL should be able to sustain payouts in the current range:

This belief is also backed up - again - by the underlying credit quality and diversification of MSDL’s total portfolio. You can see just how stable the portfolio has been if you look at MSDL’s per-share NAV figures through time.

Risks

So - we think MSDL will be able to sustain strong distributions to shareholders on the back of its outstanding credit portfolio and balance sheet. However, there are some risks that investors should be aware of when investing in MSDL.

First off, MSDL is a relatively new entity which started trading only in January of last year. This doesn’t give the firm much of a track record to draw on. It’s easy to say that the company's investments have been sound up to this point, but to some degree, the portfolio remains untested. Only a tiny fraction of assets appears delinquent, but as time goes on, things could worsen, especially if the tariff situation takes another turn.

Similarly, if you go by MSDL’s 10-Q, the company's revenues and net income are somewhat reliant on interest rates. Of course, the company boasts about its 'non-cyclical' portfolio, but in a situation where rates move down by 3%, MSDL's own internal numbers forecast income could fall by roughly $75 million.

This implies that payouts could get cut by roughly 1/3 or more. Of course, decreases in rates should increase demand for loans, so it’s likely that MSDL would take the opportunity to increase originations, but on the face of it we argue that there’s at least 25% downside to MSDL’s current payout regime in the event of rates continuing to head lower.

Summary

Overall, though, if you’re looking for a stable, double-digit dividend payer backed up by an incredibly high-quality portfolio of middle market corporate credit, it’s tough to argue that MSDL isn't a great option.

Broadly, MSDL does what it says on the tin, and should be able to sustain considerable dividends to income investors over the long haul.

Thus our 'Buy' rating.

Stay safe out there!

data.title: High-Yield Bonds: Why Shorter May Be Smarter
data.description:
By Will Smith, CFA & AJ Rivers, CFA, FRM, CAIA

Looking to reduce volatility without sacrificing income potential? Consider short-maturity high yield.

Policy uncertainty and mixed economic signals continue to upend financial markets, generating heightened volatility. While the uptick in volatility may unsettle some high-yield bond investors, we believe the sector provides continued opportunity — and that by focusing on shorter-maturity debt, investors may be able to reduce portfolio volatility without sacrificing income potential.

Higher Yields with Less Volatility: A Potent Mix

Even in normal times, a shorter-term high-yield strategy can provide high levels of income, albeit with shorter bonds yielding less than longer-dated bonds. Today, however, flatter Treasury and spread curves mean short-maturity high-yield bonds yield more than intermediate- and long-term bonds (Display). That’s a marked departure from historical norms.

Today, Shorter High-Yield Bonds Yield More than Longer Bonds

Bloomberg US Corporate High Yield Index (Percent)

What's more, yields are broadly elevated compared to historical levels of the last decade and a half. Today, yield to worst — a reliable predictor of five-year forward returns — exceeds many 10-year equity return projections. Higher yields provide a buffer against market volatility, as we saw in April, when high-yield spreads widened by roughly 60% owing to tariff concerns, but the sector nevertheless outperformed the equity market.

Short-maturity high-yield bonds also come with a more attractive risk profile than longer-term high-yield bonds, due to their lower sensitivity to changes in spread levels. Investors also have better visibility into near-term financial risks, allowing them to more easily avoid short-term bonds that might default. Typically, you’d expect to sacrifice yield in exchange for those advantages. But right now, investors in short high-yield bonds are being paid more to assume less risk.

Short Maturity Captures Less Downside

Short-maturity high-yield bonds can really show their mettle during periods of market volatility. During past equity market corrections, short-term high yield captured around three-fourths of traditional high yield’s drawdown, on average, and less than half the drawdown of equities (Display).

Shorter High Yield Has Captured Less Downside During Sell-Offs

Average Returns During Major Equity Sell-Offs, September 2000–April 2025 (Percent)

Our analysis also suggests that short-maturity high-yield bonds capture most of the broader high-yield market’s upside in risk-on environments, pointing to better potential risk-adjusted outcomes over full market cycles.

Credit Selection Matters

Of course, investors still need to be judicious about how they deploy capital, as heightened volatility can create dispersion among credit issuers. Tariffs may benefit some firms but handicap others, and slowing growth is particularly problematic for cyclicals and CCC-rated bonds — underscoring the importance of active management.

Currently, we see the best opportunities in non-cyclicals and high-yield credits rated B and above. In our view, these are better insulated from economic shocks than CCC-rated issues, which carry meaningfully higher default risk. In fact, historically, a portfolio of short-maturity BB and B-rated bonds has exhibited lower volatility than investment-grade BBB bonds.

For all these reasons, we believe that short-maturity high yield presents a timely opportunity for investors to stem volatility while capturing attractive income potential.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, and do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams and are subject to change over time.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Markets Weekly Preview: RBNZ Decision, Japan CPI, Fed Minutes, U.S. Core PCE
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Week in Review: Bitcoin all-time highs, Weaker Dollar in another volatile week

The US Dollar was once again on the main stage this week, as we have observed initial market reactions after last Friday's US Credit Rating Downgrade by Moody's Agency.

The Dollar Index (DXY) went back below the 100.00 level, longer-end yields in US Treasury Bonds went back above 5% and this Dollar weakness fueled rallies in Gold and Bitcoin, the latter breaking fresh all-time highs with a weekly high at $112,030.

Indices in North-America took a beating from the US Credit Downgrade, with both the S&P and Nasdaq down more than 2.5%. This also dragged European indices, which were playful at the beginning of the week after the EU-UK Trade Deal was achieved.

The entente was the most impactful trade deal since Brexit separated the two entities in 2020. IBEX and DAX both made new highs but got rejected, particularly after Trump's most recent tariff comments, more on this further in the article.

Bitcoin and Gold were the winners of the week, enjoying from a lower US Dollar. Both are up 4.85% as the weekly candle closes. Markets will now eye to a confirmation of new all-time highs in Bitcoin, and a retest of the last highs in the Bullion at $3,500. You can take a look at key levels for Gold here.

Currencies also all enjoyed from a fall in the Greenback, with the DXY down 1.7% on the week.

The winner was the Japan yen, closely followed by the Swissie, as markets were diversifying their Safe-Havens into other currencies.

The Euro, Pound, and even the Canadian Dollar finished up more than 1.5% against the USD. The Australian Dollar was the laggard in the race against the Greenback, with AUDUSD "only" up 1.17%.

The Week Ahead: New Zealand Rate Decision and US Core PCE and FED Minutes

The upcoming week is packed with central bank speeches kicking off over the weekend. Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell leads the lineup, with remarks scheduled for Sunday at 2:40 P.M. ET. He'll be followed on Monday by ECB President Christine Lagarde and Bank of Japan Governor Kazuo Ueda, setting the tone for the week.

Asia Pacific Markets Outlook

The Reserve Bank of New Zealand is expected to cut its Official Cash Rate from 3.50% to 3.25% on Wednesday, May 28. A new Quarterly business survey was recently published by the RBZN which sent mixed signals ahead as Inflation expectations from businesses are seen to be growing. You can access the RBZN Report here.

The Key for the meeting will be to track any neutral or hawkish tone, which would be hinting at a slower pace of cuts in the case where Inflation Expectations get out of hand.

Japan will release CPI Data on Thursday, May 29, which will be key for all markets, as all eyes have been on a Bank of Japan move. A beat on CPI may hint at future raises in the BoJ Policy Rate, which will greatly influence Carry Trades, potentially shaking markets around the world as it did in July 2024.

Last month, CPI came in at 3.5%. We will also see the release of the Japanese unemployment rate and Retail Trade data, and how both were affected by frantic US Policies.

The most recent change from the Bank of Japan was a hike to 0.50% from 0.25%, though the Central Bank has been communicating concerns about future changes due to Tariff uncertainty. The Policy Rate in Japan was negative from February 2016 until March 2024, which a change that took markets by surprise.

Europe, US and UK Market Outlook

Data releases will be limited in Europe, with mostly ECB Speakers. Markets will nonetheless expect releases for Consumer Confidence from Germany, with the last release at -20.6 and the same data for Europe expected at -15.2. These data points will be released on Tuesday, May 27.

It will be essential to see if European businesses enjoyed a more sympathetic approach by President Trump about tariffs, though his most recent comments affected the European Indices, sending the DAX down 2.7%.

FED Minutes will be released on Wednesday, and we will have a better idea of what the Federal Reserve is expecting going into future meetings.

Minutes are not the most market-moving event in the calendar, but they give more emphasis to what precise data the FED is targeting. FED Chair Powell's favorite measure until now was the Core PCE.

Talking about Core PCE, we will see the data release next Friday. The past released indicated a 2.6% Y/Y change, though the upcoming release will incorporate the most recent US PMI data and further news of the tariff's influence on core prices.

No data releases are expected from the UK, with only speeches from Bank of England Pill and Lombardelli.

Most impactful Economic Calendar releases for Next Week

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: FSCS: Low Debt A Standout Feature For This Unique Capital Strength ETF
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The First Trust SMID Capital Strength ETF (NASDAQ:FSCS) holds 100 small/mid-cap securities selected for their quality (capital strength) and risk (realized volatility) characteristics. Launched on June 20, 2017, FSCS has delivered decent returns, but my article below will reveal it as an average-at-best choice in the small/mid-cap space, given its deficiencies on other key factors like growth, momentum, and value. As such, the most appropriate rating for FSCS is a "hold," and I look forward to explaining why in further detail below.

FSCS Overview

Strategy Discussion and Peer Group

FSCS tracks the SMID Capital Strength Index, which, according to the First Trust website, selects stocks according to the following screens:

1. Eligible securities must be constituents of either the Nasdaq US Small Cap Index or the Nasdaq US Mid Cap Index. These Indexes are free-float market-cap-weighted and require companies to pass specific country, size, and liquidity screens, among others.

2. In addition, the SMID Capital Strength Index requires companies to have a least $100 million USD in cash or short-term investments, have a long-term debt to market cap ratio of less than 30%, have positive shareholder equity, and have a return on equity greater than 15%. These are the Index's "capital strength" screens.

3. Companies passing those screens are then ranked based on a combined short- and long-term (three and twelve months) realized volatility score. The top 30 companies within each ICB industry proceed to the next step.

4. The Index retains 50 small-cap and 75 mid-cap securities with the lowest combined volatility score. Finally, the 100 securities with the lowest combined volatility score qualify and are equally weighted. The Index fully rebalances and reconstitutes quarterly.

In short, the Index first screens for quality and then undergoes a series of steps to ensure adequate sector- and segment-level diversification before eliminating the most volatile securities. The result is an Index that should consistently hold high-quality, low-risk stocks, and that has enormous appeal. Small/mid-cap stocks aren't exactly known for their safety features, but it's crucial that readers know there are always sacrifices. The purpose of this article is to identify those sacrifices and maybe see if there are other ETFs that match FSCS's quality and risk features and offer something a little more. To test that, I've compiled the following peer group:

  1. First Trust SMID Growth Strength ETF (FSGS)
  2. First Trust SMID Rising Dividend Achievers ETF (SDVY)
  3. VanEck Morningstar SMID Moat ETF (SMOT)
  4. Janus Henderson Small/Mid Cap Growth Alpha ETF (JSMD)

As shown above, the First Trust ETFs all have high expense ratios, including the 0.60% annual fee for FSCS. FSGS and SDVY are two other small/mid-cap funds that appear to emphasize the growth and dividend/value factors. SMOT's "moat" focus suggests it's also a high-quality fund, and lastly, JSMD is included because it ranks pretty well in my rankings list for small/mid-cap ETFs. JSMD is also pretty well established and has an expense ratio of just 0.30%, so it could be the type of "all-weather" fund that could work for many investor types. We'll check out that hypothesis soon, but next, let's dig into FSCS's performance track record.

FSCS Performance Snapshot

Between 2018 and 2024, FSCS has delivered above-average returns in five years (2019, 2021, 2022, 2023, 2024) and below average only twice (2018, 2020) in the mid-cap blend category. The returns are pretty solid, a broad peer group includes mutual funds, many of which are easy to beat simply due to their higher fees.

Instead, it's more useful to compare FSCS against other small/mid-cap ETFs only. I track 238, 131 of which have a seven-year track record, but FSCS's 76.08% seven-year total return still ranks high at #33/131. When limited to only mid-cap ETFs, FSCS ranks #19/44, so no matter how you slice it, it's been an above-average performer.

We can also look at how FSCS compared against the peer group I selected. The chart below is from December 2017 and shows that although FSCS easily outperformed its "Growth Strength" counterpart by an annualized 2.41% (8.42% vs. 6.01%), it slightly trailed SDVY by 0.17% per year and lagged behind JSMD by 1.10% per year.

It's easy to see that JSMD took a significant lead in early 2020, but that lead has diminished in recent years, mainly due to a 22.84% loss in 2022. From the table below, you'll notice that its range of returns (best / worst years) is much higher than the other three ETFs, so it doesn't appear to consider risk much. Nevertheless, the approach has worked out well, even from a risk-adjusted returns perspective (Sharpe and Sortino Ratios), as noted above.

FSCS Analysis

Sector Allocations and Top Ten Holdings

The next table highlights the sector allocation differences between FSCS, FSGS, SDVY, SMOT, and JSMD. Notice how JSMD underweights Consumer Discretionary and Technology stocks by a combined 13%, which is likely the result of the Index's volatility screen. The main offset is in Financials and Consumer Staples, which makes it most similar to SDVY in terms of sector composition. SDVY doesn't screen for volatility, but dividend-paying stocks are often less risky, so this makes sense to me.

FSCS's top ten holdings are listed below, which total 11.44% of the fund. Since it's an equal-weighted portfolio, these are merely the best performers since the last rebalancing. For example, shares of Insulet (PODD) are up 36.02% in the last month, while UL Solutions (ULS) is up 34.75% after a fourth consecutive double-digit earnings surprise last quarter.

Fundamentals By Sub-Industry

At the sub-industry level, FSCS's top 25 comprise 72.65% of the portfolio, which is close to the concentration levels of SDVY and SMOT and about 6-11% lower than FSGS and JSMD. The following table highlights additional statistics, including the ROE and long-term debt to total market cap ratio screens used by FSCS to screen for capital strength. Have a quick look, and I'll offer some key takeaways afterward.

FSCS: Low Debt Is A Key Differentiator, FSGS Also Looks Solid

FSCS has a solid 24.28% weighted average ROE, but its 0.09 long-term debt as a percentage of market cap ratio is how it differentiates itself from most peers. As shown above, SDVY's 0.18 figure indicates its value-oriented holdings have twice as much debt as a percentage of market cap as FSCS, while SMOT and JSMD have 3-4x as much debt, with ratios of 0.36 and 0.31, respectively. Therefore, investors should have pretty good confidence that its holdings aren't overleveraged, and since leverage is one component of ROE, we also know that its selections are profitable due to the efficient use of assets (asset turnover ratio), high net margins, or both. That's not necessarily true for SDVY, SMOT, and JSMD.

Interestingly, FSGS has an even lower ratio at 0.08, and that's because its Index uses the same long-term debt-to-market cap ratio screen. You'll notice its ROE is slightly better, implying even higher asset turnover and net profit margin ratios than FSCS, and its five-year portfolio beta is only marginally higher (0.98 vs. 0.92). Per the chart below, FSGS's beta used to be much higher, so despite its poor historical returns, I think it looks a lot less risky today and is an alternative for investors seeking greater capital appreciation.

FSCS: Stable Sales and Earnings Growth Rates

On the topic of growth, I noticed that FSCS's historical three-year and estimated one-year sales and earnings growth rates are fairly stable. This is one sign that its selections are well established and have possibly entered the "mature" stage of the business life cycle. Depending on how you look at it, this could be a good or bad thing. On the one hand, it limits capital gains, but it meshes with FSCS's capital strength screens and suggests they would perform well in market downturns. To test this, I backtested FSCS's current 100-stock portfolio at equal weight, and after setting the simulation to rebalance quarterly, it produced results that defensive investors would appreciate.

As an equity ETF, FSCS is not immune from market drawdowns but had this portfolio been in place in Q1 2020 and Q1-Q3 2022, it would have outperformed the Vanguard Small-Cap ETF (VB) and the Vanguard Mid-Cap ETF (VO). This portfolio's 2022 results are especially encouraging because not only was the drawdown 10-11% better, it recovered in a mere two months. That's the main idea behind low-volatility investing and why, even absent significant growth years, the strategy can work over the long run.

FSCS: Capital Strength Comes At A Premium

FSCS trades at a relatively high valuation, especially given how it holds 30% in Financials, a sector known for its cheap P/E ratios. For example, its 16.57x forward P/E ratio is 6.23% above the peer group average, and its 12.01x trailing P/CF ratio is 10.45% above average. As shown below, FSCS's valuation ratios are above average almost across the board, and its sector-adjusted value score, which I derived from Seeking Alpha Factor Grades, also indicates it's the least attractive from a valuation perspective.

The most logical explanation is that investors are willing to pay a premium for its components' strong capital strength features, even at the expense of other factors like growth and momentum. In some cases, that makes sense, but it may not be the right move today. As I mentioned earlier, FSGS offers similar capital strength and risk features but also has 2x the estimated earnings growth at a similar price. Alternatively, more aggressive investors might consider JSMD, given its excellent growth-at-a-reasonable-price statistics and high quality, though admittedly, its components' profits are boosted by leverage. You will sacrifice some safety, but the added risk could pay off in the long run, as it has since its inception.

Investment Recommendation

FSCS selects 100 small/mid-cap companies based on their capital strength and low volatility characteristics. Its 0.60% expense ratio is high, but FSCS offers something few quality-themed ETFs have: a low long-term debt-to-market-cap ratio, which nets out at just 0.08, a fraction compared to its peers. Along with a low 0.92 five-year portfolio beta and positive backtested results, I think there's a good chance FSCS will outperform in market drawdowns. However, investors will certainly pay a premium for this added safety, and I ask readers to first consider fundamentally stronger ETFs like FSGS and even JSMD, depending on their risk tolerances. Still, FSCS deserves a solid "hold" rating, but I hope you found this information helpful, and I encourage you to reach out with any questions in the comments section below. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Trump's 50% Tariff Threat To The EU: Deal Or Detonation?
data.description:
By Inga Fechner | Carsten Brzeski | Chris Turner

EU’s trade offers meet 50% tariff threat

Despite the EU's efforts to secure a deal by delaying its countermeasures for 90 days until 14 July, and by offering more than only cosmetic deal proposals, the US administration seems to be dissatisfied. The EU has offered a €50 billion trade deal that includes LNG purchases and soybeans to reduce the trade deficit, proposing 0% tariffs on cars and industrial goods, or revising its proposal by offering strategic cooperation in energy, artificial intelligence, and digital infrastructure and commitments to international labour rights, environmental standards, and economic security. It remains unclear what the US administration actually expects in order to still strike a deal, other than the earlier contemplated unilateral reduction of EU tariffs.

What do the facts say?

While average tariffs between the US and the EU are similar, sectoral differences are indeed stark. Take, for example, higher EU tariffs on cars (10% vs. 2.5% in the US) and food. But the US has its own steep barriers. Light-trucks have long-faced a 25% tariff rate, compared to 10% in the EU. Regarding the trade balance, the EU enjoyed a €198.2bn surplus with the US in 2024. Yet, when looking at services, the US runs a €109bn surplus (as of 2023), with notable IT service exports, led by dominant American tech companies, charges for intellectual property and financial services.

Is a deal still possible?

Despite the aggressive rhetoric, Trump’s tariff threats are often a prelude to negotiation, as with China’s weekend deal at the beginning of May (which in fact was more a delay than a real deal). However, it has always been clear that the US administration’s stance on the EU will be different than with most other trading partners. Comments like 'the EU is nastier than China' have characterised the US administration’s approach so far. And while it is hard to see what the US administration’s endgame could be, the EU’s approach is clear. It follows a two-pronged strategy: signal openness to a deal while preparing for escalation. While the EU is slower to act, it has prepared a couple of retaliatory tariff measures which are currently scheduled to enter into force on 14 July. And if talks truly collapse, expect the EU to reach for its heaviest artillery, such as tighter regulations on US tech firms, delaying licenses or restricting public procurement access and limiting IP rights and investment flows under the Anti-Coercion Instrument (ACI).

Volatility reloaded

A relatively calm week comes to an abrupt and disappointing close, serving as a stark reminder that we remain far from any 'beautiful trade deals' or a return to normality.

Volatility is back. And just to be sure, if fully implemented, 50% US tariffs on European products would shave off some 0.6ppt of GDP growth and bring the eurozone economy close to recession territory. Needless to say, it would also increase stagflationary pressures in the US again.

At the current juncture, it doesn’t make sense to speculate on the outcome of these trade tensions. Anything is possible, from a potential agreement to further escalation. We maintain our long-held view that, in time, the economic toll of an escalating trade war on the US will become too severe to sustain elevated tariffs indefinitely. After a brief lull, brace yourself for the next turbulent chapter.

European markets adjust

Even though the 50% tariff may be just be a temporary threat, European equity markets quickly traded lower on the news. The Euro Stoxx 50 index is off close to 3% at the time of writing, with the auto sector off 4-5%. Two-year EUR swap rates are some 6bp lower as the market prices in an extra 10bp to this year’s European Central Bank easing cycle – now priced at a further 62bp of cuts for the year.

EUR/USD briefly traded 0.6% lower on the news, but has bounced back a little – presumably on doubts about whether these 50% tariffs ever see the light of day. Today’s developments have also seen EUR/JPY come lower on the view that the yen, rather than the euro, will prove the prime beneficiary of any dollar outflows this summer. And EUR/GBP has traded to the lowest levels since early April now that the UK has a trade deal.

Today’s events are certainly a reminder to investors not to be too complacent over the tariff story this summer. Both June and July could be rocky for markets – and could keep traded FX volatility high – as Washington pressures the EU and the rest of the world into trade deals.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Nvidia: 5 Channel Checks Ahead Of Earnings
data.description:
The most important company in the world right now is Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA), and it’s set to announce earnings on Wednesday, May 28th after the close. Governments, other companies (large and small), and billions of people all rely increasingly on Nvidia’s computer chips, directly and indirectly. And considering the massive Artificial Intelligence ("AI") megatrend, demand for Nvidia’s Hopper (H100 and H200) and Blackwell chips dramatically exceeds supply (as incredible value-adding use cases are only just beginning to be discovered). This report reviews Nvidia’s business, growth potential, five important channel checks (suggesting Nvidia will surpass its revenue and EPS guidance), valuation, big risks, and then concludes with my strong opinion on investing.

Business Overview:

Nvidia is basically ground zero for the AI megatrend, a very large market opportunity that is already dominated by Nvidia (roughly 85-90% of all AI is run on Nvidia chips), and that market is expected to grow at nearly a 36% ("CAGR") through 2030.

Specifically, Nvidia’s graphics processing units, or GPUs (aka "chips"), dominate in data centers, gaming, and automotive sectors, and the data center segment alone (basically where cloud AI happens) generated $39.3 billion in revenue last quarter (over 80% of Nvidia’s total revenue). Further, Nvidia’s CUDA software platform strengthens its AI ecosystem ("moat") with partnerships across hyperscalers and neocloud providers (more on these partners later). And Nvidia's robotics efforts increases the market opportunity dramatically further.

So with that backdrop in mind, let’s get into some Nvidia channel checks suggesting growth remains extremely robust.

Channel Check 1: Hyperscaler AI Investments

The AI hyperscalers (i.e. large scale cloud service providers with massive compute requirements) are demonstrating the demand for Nvidia chips is still accelerating.

For example, Meta Platforms (META) (a big Nvidia customer) just recently raised its 2025 capex estimate to $64–$72 billion, citing “hard-to-meet” AI compute needs. And Amazon (AMZN) continues to expand its collaboration with Nvidia on AI for services like Bedrock. Further, Microsoft’s (MSFT) $80 billion FY2025 AI data center budget (with 52% capex growth) is driving demand for H100 GPUs in its Azure cloud platform. Further still, Elon Musk (TSLA) recently announced that his xAI will be implementing 1 million GPUs outside of Memphis (mostly Nvidia chips, some AMD chips) which basically equates to a $30-40B purchase order to Nvidia.

All of this recent information bodes well for Nvidia’s upcoming earnings announcement.

Channel Check 2: Neocloud Provider Growth

As another indication that Nvidia chip orders are outpacing forecasts, the neocloud providers (offering Nvidia GPU-based cloud services) are scaling very quickly.

For example, CoreWeave (CRWV), which started trading publicly in March, just reported first quarter revenue of $0.98B (420.3% year-over-year growth) with 70% tied to hyperscalers leasing Nvidia GPUs. CoreWeave also has a revenue backlog of $25.9B, and is in advanced talks with Alphabet (GOOGL) to lease Nvidia Blackwell chips to Alphabet.

Also, Nebius Group (NBIS), another neocloud provider, just reported $55.3 million in revenue (+385% year over year), driven primarily by the core AI infrastructure business (i.e. Nvidia). Further, private firms like Lambda, Crusoe, Nscale and DeepNight are all expanding. This all bodes well for Nvidia.

Channel Check 3: Wall Street Analysts

Analysts appear increasingly optimistic about Nvidia’s upcoming earnings. For example, Morgan Stanley recently noted a rise in Nvidia’s rack shipments to 1,500 in April from 1,000 in Q1, projecting 73% data center revenue growth. Also, Loop Capital reiterated a Buy rating on Nvidia noting that “Blackwell's autumn ramp up expectations will carry the stock.” And Raymond James just reiterated its strong buy rating on the shares. And as you can see in the image below, Wall Street analysts rate the shares a “strong buy” and suggest they are more than 20% undervalued (see the "price to consensus target" column in the valuation table / section of this report).

Channel Check 4: TSM’s AI Chip Production

Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), Nvidia’s key foundry (i.e., outsourced chip producer), reported a Q1 2025 year-over-year revenue increase of 41.6% (in NT), while net income and diluted EPS increased 60.3% and 60.4%, respectively. And CEO C.C. Wei highlighted “insatiable” orders for Nvidia’s Blackwell GPUs. In fact, the company is now expected to spend between $38 billion and $42 billion on capex for 2025 (up from $29.2 billion in 2024); a good sign for the industry and Nvidia growth.

And the company’s April sales (up 22.2% month-over-month, and 48.1% year-over-year) suggests the AI infrastructure ramp remains quite healthy (a good sign for Nvidia’s future).

Channel Check 5: Geopolitical AI Demand

Geopolitical dynamics are also boosting Nvidia’s demand. For example, Saudi Arabia’s new $20 billion AI chip deal with Nvidia (along with a new AI “factory” partnership) underscore Middle Eastern interest in AI and Nvidia. Also, U.S. onshoring efforts (backed by the Trump administration) have prompted Nvidia’s “several hundred billion” U.S.-manufacturing commitment (also leveraging TSM’s $100 billion U.S. investment). So even though the U.S. has imposed export restrictions of Nvidia chips to China (costing an estimated $5.5 billion in Q1), the business remains very much alive, well and growing from a bigger picture level.

Nvidia’s Current Valuation:

As mentioned, Nvidia remains very highly rated (strong buy) by Wall Street analysts (as you can see in the table below). It is extremely profitable (55.9% net margin), and remains compelling on a forward PEG (price/earnings to growth) ratio, especially considering its high growth, dominant market position, and the long legs of the ongoing AI megatrend. For reference, you can also see several of the AI hyperscalers and neocloud providers mentioned earlier in the table below.

Risks:

Of course, Nvidia also faces significant risks that investors need to consider. For example:

Geopolitics is perhaps Nvidia’s biggest risk right now, ranging from tariffs (as mentioned earlier) to international supply chain issues (the industry is highly cyclical and prone to delays resulting from supply and demand imbalances). Nvidia already recently announced a $5.5B hit from China tariffs (as mentioned), and considering U.S. onshoring, Middle East efforts, and the volatile tariff dynamics (considering who is in the White House) more volatility may persist.

Hyperscaler Concentration is another big risk. For example, 50% of Nvidia’s revenue comes from just a few clients, and any signs of spending slow-downs could cause the shares to fall. However, as mentioned in the hyperscaler section of this report, the current growth environment continues to look promising, especially considering the healthy growth trajectory of the AI megatrend.

Competition: As mentioned, Nvidia dominates the AI GPU market with overwhelming market share and strengthened by its ecosystem (CUDA) moat. Nonetheless, there is competition from Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) and ongoing efforts by hyperscalers to develop in house chips. Not to mention the threat of new compute technologies, such as DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK), which could dramatically shift the demand for Nvidia chips (i.e., enabling higher compute with less GPUs). Nonetheless, Nvidia remains the clear industry leader, fueling the overwhelming majority of the dominant AI megatrend.

Conclusion:

It’s been a particularly volatile high-growth period for Nvidia, and the company is set to announce earnings on Wednesday, May 28th (after the close). Considering the strength of the AI megatrend (as described in the channel checks above) it seems likely for Nvidia to surpass the street’s ~$43B revenue and ~$0.87 earnings-per-share estimates. Not to mention, CEO Jensen Huang is a superstar at prudently and conservatively managing expectations (the company frequently outperforms). The real question will be any new insights on the short-term impacts of geopolitics (tariffs), the rate of acceleration for the Blackwell ramp (by how much is demand exceeding supply?) and the ongoing strength of the longer-term AI megatrend (which is perhaps one of the most profoundly disruptive technologies in some time). Long Nvidia.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Microsoft: This Might Be The Top
data.description:
Although Microsoft’s (NASDAQ:MSFT) stock has slightly increased since we published our latest article on the business in November, we continue to think that the company is overvalued at the current price. We also think that Microsoft’s desire to increase its capital spending to record levels to expand its AI capabilities could backfire due to the rising macro risks that can undermine the potential of its AI projects. As such, we continue to rate the company as a SELL.

Some Good Things To Consider First

While we’re bearish about Microsoft right now, it’s still important to note that the company nevertheless has some good things going for it right now. Just a few weeks ago, Microsoft reported its Q3 results, which positively surprised the market as revenues of $70.06 billion were above expectations by $1.61 billion, while GAAP EPS of $3.46 was also above the consensus by $0.24.

The productivity and business processes division managed to generate $29.9 billion in revenues, up 10% Y/Y, while the personal computing division generated $13.4 billion in revenues, up 6% Y/Y.

Back in January, Gartner forecasted that the global IT spending could reach record levels in 2025 and spending on software is forecasted to grow at a double-digit rate. If that’s the case, then Microsoft’s core business might have additional room for growth.

Also, the cloud business showed exceptional results in Q1, as its revenues of $26.8 billion were up 21% Y/Y. Considering the strengths of Azure, it’s possible for it to continue to show a decent performance in the foreseeable future.

The Macro Risks Are Real

Despite all the recent positive developments, there’s a case to be made that Microsoft’s downside is more significant, and its stock has room to fall.

For starters, Gartner’s forecast was released back in January before the global trade war by the Trump administration was initially launched. While there has been a pause on the implementation of reciprocal tariffs on most countries by the Trump administration in April, there’s still uncertainty about what’s going to happen next and what the updated trade policy might look like in the future.

Just recently, President Trump announced that he’s going to recommend a 50% tariff on goods imported from the European Union starting June 1, which is before the date when the 90-day pause should expire. Such uncertainty regarding the trade policy makes it harder for businesses and consumers to forecast their spending, which might hurt economic activity.

During the latest conference call, the retail giant Walmart (WMT) already announced the possibility of higher prices for the products that it sells due to the current trade policy. Also, consumer sentiment has now fallen to its 3-year low, while the Federal Reserve hasn’t fully dismissed the stagflation scenario in the latest meeting.

When it comes to software spending in particular, the report that was published by the Boston Consulting Group earlier this month showed that there has been a significant decline in the desire of CIOs to increase their IT budgets this year, mostly because of tariffs. The majority of CIOs now also believe that the recession is likely this year. As a result of this, Gartner’s January forecast on spending might not materialize by the end of the year as risks increase. Therefore, while Microsoft had a great performance in Q3, which is also a period where no major tariffs were announced, there’s now a risk that the sales might suffer in Q4 and beyond as businesses begin to adapt to the new macro environment.

Are We In A Bubble?

Besides the macro issues, there’s a possibility that there’s an overcapacity of data centers, and the expected record spending on AI-related initiatives by Microsoft and its peers might lead to an overbuilding within the industry and depress future earnings.

In late March, it was reported that Microsoft had decided to abandon some data center projects due to the oversupply. Then Alibaba’s (BABA) CEO Joe Tsai warned of a potential AI data center bubble, while Wells Fargo reported that Amazon is pausing some of its lease commitments as well. The news from China also indicates that due to the aggressive build-out, many data centers now sit idly and companies have started to sell their GPUs so that they can somewhat offset the oversupply issues.

While there is no way to figure out how many data centers around the globe are unused, the fact that the semiconductor sales have been below their peak levels for a few months might suggest that the oversupply issues are real.

The problem though is that even after abandoning some of its own projects a few months ago, Microsoft’s management during the latest conference call committed once again to spending the record CapEx this year.

This could become a major problem for Microsoft and its peers as it could lead to overbuilding within the industry, which already faces oversupply issues. Let’s not forget that in the late 1990s, the telecom industry also poured capital into massive projects only to yield minimal or no returns at all. As the former Under Secretary of Commerce for Economic Affairs Mark Domes noted in one of his works:

… the telecom service sector went on a capital expenditure binge … capital investment by publicly traded telecom service companies rose sharply in the late 1990s, starting at $47 billion in 1995 and peaking at $121 billion in 2000. Since then, however, the telecom service industry landscape is littered with the wrecks of overly optimistic expectations, as witnessed by the bankruptcies by WorldCom, Global Crossing, and numerous smaller firms.

What’s also important to note is that the overbuilding also tends to depress the future earnings. Here’s how American economists Robin Greenwood and Samuel Hanson explain this:

… heavy investment during booms predictably depresses future earnings and the price of capital, leading prices to overshoot their rational-expectations levels … particularly in markets—such as industries with long time-to-build delays—where feedback is delayed and learning is slow.

This shows that Microsoft’s upside might be limited, as the record capital spending in the current environment could yield limited returns.

The Intrinsic Value of Microsoft

The rising macro risks and the expected higher capital spending prompted us to change several assumptions in our valuation model, which last year showed Microsoft’s intrinsic value to be $324.16 per share.

Our valuation model on Microsoft is updated at a time when the company trades at $458.17 per share. The cash and total debt numbers that are presented in the new model have been taken from the recent earnings report, while the perpetual growth rate here is 3%, the same as in the previous model. The 3% also makes the most sense, as it’s close to the historical GDP and inflation rates.

The discount rate in the model is 7.66%. We figured it out by calculating Microsoft’s cost of debt using mostly its TTM results, and by calculating the company’s cost of equity where the risk-free rate is 4.52%, beta is 1.02, and the market return rate is 7.77%.

In the table below, we still forecast the growth of Microsoft’s sales and EBIT margin as the company is less likely to be significantly impacted by tariffs in comparison to other companies from the Magnificent Seven group like Apple (AAPL) or Amazon (AMZN). However, the growth rate nevertheless is expected to be slightly lower in comparison to the previous model. This is because there’s a possibility that the potential negative impact of the global trade war risks decreasing the demand for the company’s products and services. The sales assumptions for the next couple of years are also not that far away from the overall consensus.

The assumptions for the tax rate have been lowered to 15%, which could become a new standard corporate rate in the United States, while the assumptions for the D&A and the change in net working capital mostly remained the same as before.

At the same time, the CapEx expectations have been greatly increased. This is because Microsoft’s management earlier this year said that CapEx in Q3 and Q4 should be similar to the Q2 levels. In Q3, the CapEx of $16.7 billion was only slightly above the Q2 levels. At the same time, during the latest conference call the management reiterated that the expectations so far haven’t changed significantly but CapEx in Q4 is still expected to be up Q/Q. We have reflected these capital spending expectations for the current fiscal year in our model.

In the end, our valuation model showed that Microsoft’s enterprise value is $2.35 trillion, its equity value is $2.32 trillion, and its intrinsic value is $311.60 per share, down around 32% from the current price.

Final Thoughts

The amount of risks that Microsoft faces has been on the rise and will likely continue to increase due to the worsening of the macro environment. The company’s desire to drastically increase its capital spending in the current environment is also a bad idea, in our opinion, since the return on that spending might yield limited returns. As such, Microsoft’s shares, in our opinion, have room to fall, especially since the company remains overvalued at the current price. That is why Microsoft remains a SELL for us.

data.title: Stellantis: Margin Recovery Catalysts, Initiate Buy
data.description:
Key thesis

We initiate coverage on Stellantis N.V. (NYSE: NYSE:STLA) with a Buy rating and a $14 PT. STLA designs, manufactures, and markets a broad range of automobiles, commercial vehicles, and related parts and services in global markets. Our Buy thesis is based on our FY25E EBITDA of €22.4bn and FY26E EBITDA of €23.3bn, +5.6% and +5% ahead of consensus, on product launches, execution of the €10bn cost program, and accelerating Latin America momentum, up 150bps of share in 1Q25. We see a significant market disconnect on STLA's earnings power, with shares trading at 0.73x forward EV/EBITDA (EV based on prevailing market cap and net debt, and our in-house FY26E EBITDA forecast), vs. peers. Our PT uses 1.70x FY26E EV/EBITDA, which is still conservative relative to history/peers, but reflects both operational progress made and embedded structural risk.

NA pricing normalization and diminishing dealer inventory provide the backdrop for margin inflection and cost absorption into 2H25, alongside vehicle launches, including the Citroën C3, Dodge Charger, and Jeep Wagoneer S, providing incremental upside as they reach full availability. Tariff policy uncertainty and CEO transition are priced into our valuation. We see a favorable R/R, with real catalysts for earnings outperformance and valuation normalization over our forecast period.

Stellantis: Modeling EBITDA Outperformance with Resilient Upstream Insulation

We model FY25E/FY26E EBITDA of €22.4bn/€23.3bn (+5.6%/+5% above the Street consensus) as disciplined execution on €10bn cost-out program & high-impact model launches catalyzing earnings resilience. Key cost-out milestones include the capture of €2.5–3bn of annualized run-rate savings by end-2025. We expect increased visibility over ≤€650–750mn quarterly cost-out captured in SG&A/procurement, with concrete progress already factored into a 29% US dealer inventory reduction in H2 2024. We should see a steady decline of fixed costs & SG&A-to-sales ratios in quarterly P&Ls, with visible milestones including inventory normalization, production schedules & lower recourse to cash.

On the product side, management forecasts targeting >300k units of incremental annual volume (AV) impact from key ramps (Citroën C3, Dodge Charger, Jeep Wagoneer S) by end-2025 guide forecasts of group market share stabilization in Europe & an evidenced mix shift towards B/C segments. We model robust product ramps to more than offset industry headwinds, contributing >€2bn to group FY25E revenues and, combined with scale & platform sharing, at least 60–80bps to group EBITDA margin by FY26E due to the full-year difference, including acceleration in Smart Car platform and midsize BEVs.

The full-year impact of key launches supports the conviction that Stellantis can deliver material margin recovery against a backdrop of industry-wide headwinds. The improving regional portfolio, notably South America, with Q1 2025 market share up 1.5ppt to 23.8%, should deliver resilience to the group and offset the North American AM piece pricing resets to support above-consensus IAS 17 core earnings.

Inventories now at healthy levels across regions should help drive a continued improvement in WC and heightened quarterly visibility. Risks from US tariffs and CEO succession are acknowledged, although conservatively incorporated into our forecasts. We believe to have cost-out milestones tracked and bottom-up analyses of the volumes/margin impact from key race launches to provide visibility & opacify Street estimates on core EBITDA & EPS line through FY26.

Source: FMP FY25 FY26
Estimate # Analysts Estimate # Analysts
Revenue $174.24B 14 $182.14B 14
EBITDA $21.21B 14 $22.17B 14
Net Income $5.92B 14 $7.80B 14
EPS $1.99 4 $2.62 3
SG&A Expenses $9.94B 14 $10.39B 14

Cost Takeout and Margin Inflection: The Key to a Re-Rating

A credible margin inflection arguably remains the most important catalyst for STLA at this stage, as the group emerged from a wobbly 2024 (-64% adj. Op. income; AOI margin bottoming at the bottom of the 5.5–7% range). The €10bn cost takeout is not just a headline number: its tangible delivery can already be seen in the aggressive headcount restructuring (which was €1.6bn in 2024, and we expect to taper going forward, providing a front-loaded benefit to recurring cost savings). Equally important, STLA's normalization of NA dealer inventory levels (from 430k midyear to 304k YE, vs 330k target) has reset the baseline for production and pricing discipline, removing the need for margin-dilutive incentives and forced production cuts.

This operational reset is set to manufacturing utilization and FCF, with management explicitly guiding to mid-single-digit AOI margins and a return to positive industrial FCF in 2025 as plant loading and cost absorption improve with the return of volumes in H2. In our view, successful cost discipline and normalized inventory levels are the bedrock not only for near-term margin inflection but for a re-rating of STLA as it demonstrates the ability to generate sustainable cash and earnings above depressed expectations.

Valuation

Our $14 PT is derived by applying a 1.70x EV/EBITDA multiple to our FY26E EBITDA forecast of €23.3bn, reflecting a balanced mix of operational upside and risk discount. The target multiple is materially below the historical median (2.0x) and below the peer group median (2.3x) based on a trailing 8-year dataset across global OEMs (1st–99th percentile range), sourced from FMP. This implies a historical/relative percentile (1st, 0th) near absolute trough and a significant margin of safety. The multiple is warranted by our conviction in company-specific catalysts around the execution of the €10bn cost-reduction plan, and positive follow-through from new model launches, which underpins our above-consensus FY26E EBITDA expansion of +4.0%. These structural cost improvements, combined with North American pricing normalization and ongoing inventory drawdowns, justify a measured normalization in the company's multiple without dismissing ongoing uncertainty around tariffs and succession planning.

Importantly, while market narratives remain focused on Stellantis' cyclical exposure and headline EV transition risk, we argue that the Street consensus underestimates the scale of margin leverage from cost disciplines already in place, an operational dynamic largely overlooked but now apparent in Latin America's market share gains. Ultimately, our valuation captures the interplay of risk adj. and margin catalyst, offering a compelling R/R anchored in tangible operational catalysts.

Downside risks to our Buy recommendation

While we see a compelling margin recovery and multiple re-rating opportunities in Stellantis, our Buy thesis could be undermined by lingering execution headwinds or macro developments. Key risks remain under close watch:

• Tariff regime uncertainty and trade policy evolution, particularly in the US, with newly implemented and prospective tariffs on imported vehicles and key inputs threatening NA operations, pricing flexibility, or cost structure, with management itself citing suspended visibility and continuing “tariff-related uncertainties” as a reason for withdrawing guidance for FY25E.

• Execution of a restructuring program and labor relations, with the group’s ambitious €10bn cost reduction program at risk of stalling in the face of labor tensions (notably in the US, where dealer dissatisfaction is rising and profit warnings/revived union strike threats are coming out in the media), especially if management's new approach/leadership does not succeed in calming unrest and segment overcapacity.

• Fierce EV transition ahead, with accelerating Chinese EV imports in Europe/NA and low elasticity for BEVs in the face of policy flux and slower-than-anticipated electrified model growth, constituting a structural risk requiring additional pricing action or capping volume/margin expansion if not proactively managed.

In summary, if tariffs unexpectedly escalate, inventory normalization falters, or the competitive/EV transition landscape worsens, our forecast of rapid EBITDA outperformance and re-rating is at risk. Management’s ability to navigate these cross-currents, retain a disciplined cost trajectory, and claw back NA volume share are the most important areas to watch for risk to our Buy thesis.

Conclusion

We initiate with a Buy on STLA, given the rare alignment of NT margin catalysts and Op momentum (including accelerating leverage from cost-discipline and new model launches) that we believe is materially underappreciated by the market, alongside unbalanced conservatism around a valuation framework that offers a favorable risk/reward vs. cyclicality and structural risks. We recommend using volatility to add to exposure, with management cost execution and NA share recovery providing the visibility to upside.

data.title: TD Bank Beats Q2 Earnings, Pledges More Buybacks (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
The Toronto-Dominion Bank (NYSE:TD)(TSX:TD:CA) released its second quarter earnings Yesterday. The release beat analyst estimates on revenue as well as on earnings, with revenue coming in a full $1.7 billion ahead of estimates. The company's U.S. retail segment was again a weak point, with earnings down 76%. However, the results in all other segments--Canadian retail, insurance, and capital markets--were excellent.

TD's Q2 release came following several months of TD stock outperforming the broader market. The stock was already up for the year when its earnings release came out; it is now up 27% year to date.

I was pretty optimistic heading into TD's earnings release. I knew that the bank had good prospects in several segments, such as wholesale banking (i.e. investment banking) and Canadian retail. Investment banking fees have been rising this year, albeit at a slower pace than last year, and Canadian mortgage rates remain fairly high, despite the Bank of Canada's recent rate cuts. For these reasons, I thought that TD had a decent shot at beating on earnings.

Despite this, I was still surprised by the strength of the results that TD actually put out. I figured that the U.S. retail segment would hold overall results back enough that growth would be pretty minimal. Instead, TD actually delivered its best growth rates in many quarters. The bank also pledged to continue its ongoing buyback program, which it initiated after selling off its Charles Schwab (SCHW) stake to comply with the asset cap that the Department of Justice ((DoJ)) imposed on it late last year.

When I last covered TD Bank, I rated the stock a buy on the grounds that it was making heady progress in recovering from its anti-money laundering (AML) scandal and associated penalties. Since then, the bank has gone on to perform even better than I expected--and my expectations were pretty high. While TD stock is up 13% since my article published, I'm actually even more bullish on it now than I was then, because of the company's strong earnings performance. Accordingly, I am upgrading my TD rating to strong buy.

Earnings Recap

TD Bank's second quarter earnings release was quite strong, boasting the following metrics:

  • Revenue: C$15.14 billion, up 9.1% year over year (beat by C$1.7 billion).
  • Net interest income (NII): $8.125 billion, up 8.8%.
  • Reported EPS: C$6.28, up 305%.
  • Adjusted EPS: C$1.97, down 2.4% (beat by $0.19).
  • A 9 million share reduction.
  • A 14.9 CET1 ratio.
  • A 12.4% return on equity.
  • A 15% return on tangible equity.
  • A 4.7 leverage ratio.

For the most part, the metrics that TD announced in its second quarter earnings release were outstanding. The company grew its revenue, NII and reported earnings; realized a nice one-time gain on its Schwab stake; and also delivered high capital adequacy and liquidity.

One thing definitely wasn't so hot in TD's second quarter release, though: the U.S. retail segment's performance. The segment's net income was $120 million, down 76%. That was not such a great showing, although nobody was really expecting U.S. retail to grow--the $430 billion asset cap threw cold water on any possibility of that happening.

The Canadian retail segment's showing was mixed, with revenue up 3% but earnings down 4%

Wealth Management and Insurance did well, with revenue up 12% and earnings up 14%.

Last but not least, wholesale banking (essentially investment banking) achieved solid results, with revenue up 10%, reported earnings up 16% and adjusted earnings up 1%. The investment bank's showing was particularly impressive considering that Donald Trump's on-and-off tariffs have been having a chilling effect on M&A activity.

Future Prospects

Having explored TD's recent quarterly results, we can now turn to evaluating its future prospects. For this, we need to look at the earnings call transcript.

CEO Raymond Chun said on TD's earnings call that forecasting future results would be difficult because of the macroeconomic uncertainty Donald Trump's tariff policies created. He noted the U.S.-China tariff deal as a positive, but also said that U.S.-Canada trade disagreements remained. U.S. operations head Leo Salom said that the company had been making progress with its AML remediation program, drawing on AI and other advanced technologies to identify crimes like money laundering before they occur. These measures increase costs but decrease the probability of TD taking another AML fine; on balance they will likely have a positive effect on TD's future earnings.

The only specific numerical guidance that TD gave on its earnings call was for NII, which it said that it expected to increase by C$300 million to C$500 million for the year. Assuming that tax rates and other such items don't change, then, TD's earnings will probably go up by at least C$300 million. In fact, they will likely go up more, as earnings in 2024's fourth quarter were impacted by a C$3 billion fine. The lack of a fine in 2025's fourth quarter will presumably have a positive effect on earnings growth for the whole year. For this reason, I would expect TD's earnings for 2025 to be equivalent to last year's plus C$3.5 billion, so roughly C$12.3 billion, or C$7 per share on an adjusted basis.

Valuation

Having explored TD's recent earnings and future prospects, we can now proceed to valuing the shares. We can start with the multiples that Seeking Alpha Quant has on file.

As you can see, TD scores a respectable 'B' on valuation in Seeking Alpha's quant rankings. Some standout metrics include the 9.6 GAAP P/E ratio and the 0.15 PEG ratio. These are cheaper than what is typical for North American money center banks today.

On the other hand, I estimated earlier that TD would earn C$7.06 in earnings per share for 2025. The TSX-listed version of TD trades at C$92.91, so that's a 13.16 P/E ratio if TD earns what I expect it to. This is higher than the multiples Seeking Alpha Quant has on file. However, it's still relatively low.

What kind of multiple does TD deserve to trade at? We can approach that with discounted cash flows. The 10-year treasury currently has a 4.5% yield. Discounting my $7.06 EPS estimate of 4.5% produces a C$155 price target, or $113 for the U.S. listed shares.

Now, TD is not risk-free, so we should probably throw in a risk premium here. Using a 3% risk premium (a 7.5% discount rate), TD is worth C$94 or $68.71. There remains only a small amount of upside here. However, this is all based on a no-growth assumption. I think TD will probably continue growing long term, and that the growth will make it worth more than what the calculations above indicate.

The Bottom Line

The bottom line on TD's second quarter earnings release is that it exceeded expectations on every metric. Not only did the bank beat estimates for the quarter just reported, its executives also said that they expect positive growth in NII for the current fiscal year. Based on this, I'm expecting good things from TD in the year ahead. So, I rate the stock a strong buy.
